steps.title: Pin down what the claim requires
steps.content: The underlined claim is that olms "regularly come to the surface to perform important activities such as finding food." Supporting evidence must directly connect surface visits to food-finding behavior — not other activities, geography, or anatomy.

steps.title: Test each choice against the claim
steps.content:
• Choice A — Researchers determine that olms don't breed often.: Breeding frequency says nothing about surfacing to find food. ✗
  • Choice B — Researchers confirm that olms live in only a few cave systems.: Geographic range of habitat doesn't explain why olms surface or whether they do so to eat. ✗
  • Choice C — Researchers discover that earthworms from surface soils are a major part of olms' diet.: Earthworms live on the surface, not in underwater caves. If they make up a major part of olms' diet, olms must be surfacing to get them — directly supporting the claim. ✓
  • Choice D — Researchers learn that olms' brains differ from other salamanders' brains.: Brain anatomy has no clear connection to surfacing for food. ✗
That matches Choice C — Researchers discover that earthworms from surface soils are a major part of olms' diet..
